Warren Wilkinson

home

Expected Value of a Continuous Uniform Distribution

11 Aug 2013

Table of Contents

  • 1 Inputting Greek Characters
  • 2 Inputting Formulas
  • 3 Density Function and Probability
  • 4 Expected Value

Inputting Greek Characters

I've been told that the variance of a continuous uniform distribution is something. Now I want to prove it; and learn more about emacs.

So first… variance.. 

M-x set-input-method RET TeX

Now I can write \sigma^2 to produce σ². Which is the variance, go me.

However, I notice when I export, ''\sigma^2' gets converted to σ². I don't want that, because I'm writing the greek symbols directly as UTF8 in my file, so I turn that off with 

#+OPTIONS: ^:nil, TeX:nil

Now, I'm pretty happy, but it's a bit annoying that my input mode bothers me each time I type a \, -, ^, or _. Luckily I can press C-\ to turn it off and on. I'm happy.

(Later, I found that if I'd let Org mode do the conversion, I could inline x_{max} and have it expanded. There are no greek letters for tiny m-a-x, so my method is inferior).

Inputting Formulas

Can I write the formula? MathML or something? I can use: 

\begin{equation}
x=\sqrt{b}
\end{equation}

And I get:

\begin{equation} x=\sqrt{b} \end{equation}

Again, happy.

Density Function and Probability

Okay, here is what my book tells me:

In a uniform distribution, x has a density function

\begin{equation} f(x) = \frac{1}{x_{max} - x_{min}} \end{equation}

So what's x? I guess the domain. Fine. So lets pretend we've got a machine that randomly picks points on the line between (3,0) and (5,0). X would be between 3 and 5.

So the probability of the point being between points a and b is:

\begin{equation} P_{ab} = \frac{(b -a)}{(x_{max} - x_{min})} \end{equation}

of an region along that line.

It's pretty easy to compute: Basically take the sum of the probabilities of all the points between A and B. In a continuous situation, that's an integral.

\begin{equation} P_{ab} = \int_a^b f(x) \mathrm{d} x = \int_a^b \frac{1}{x_{max} - x_{min}} \mathrm{d}x \end{equation}

Notice that f(x) is actually a constant that doesn't depend on x at all…

\begin{equation} P_{ab} = \frac{1}{x_{max} - x_{min}} \int_a^b 1 \mathrm{d}x \end{equation} \begin{equation} P_{ab} = \frac{1}{x_{max} - x_{min}}\Bigg [_a^b x \end{equation} \begin{equation} P_{ab} = \frac{b -a}{x_{max} - x_{min}} \end{equation}

Wow, I made several mistakes writing out the LaTeX for that. I know org mode can preview those equations (as images) as I write them. I'll learn how to turn that on, next time, so I can minimize the number of typing->output trips I need to make.

Okay, that's the easy stuff.

Expected Value

So, the expected value E(x) = μ is the mean (the symbols represent the same thing). In other words – if we randomly picked a hundred thousand billion numbers, and averaged them all together, what would we expect the result to be.

Since I'm a big fan of actually trying things, lets do that. 

;; Put cursor on closing paren and
;; press C-x C-e to evaluate

(defvar *x-min* 3.0)
(defvar *x-max* 5.0)

;; Emacs doesn't have random floating
;; point numbers. So approximate it 
;;...

;(defun pick-number ()
;  (+ (random (- *x-max* *x-min*)) *x-min*))

(defun pick-number ()
  (+ (/ (random (* (floor (- *x-max* *x-min*)) 1000000)) 1000000.0) *x-min*))

;; Use C-u C-x C-e to evaluate and print results

(pick-number)3.053389
(pick-number)4.5719829999999995
(pick-number)3.5274710000000002

(defun take-average (n)
  (let ((accumulator 0))
    (dotimes (x n (/ accumulator n))
      (incf accumulator (pick-number)))))

(take-average 100)3.9764163599999995
(take-average 1000)3.9871245689999983)
(take-average 10000)4.003674864100012

Wild guess: E(x) = 4.

Anyway, a better way would be to actually take a weighted average of all the probabilities.

\begin{equation} E(x) = \int_3^5 xf(x) \mathrm{d}x = \ldots \end{equation}

And once again f(x) is actually a constant that doesn't depend on x at all (but that won't be true of other distributions!!!), so we'll just call it C.

\begin{equation} C = \frac{1}{5-3} = \frac{1}{2} \end{equation} \begin{equation} E(x) = C \int_3^5 x \mathrm{d}x \end{equation} \begin{equation} E(x) = C \Bigg[_3^5 \frac{x^2}{2} \end{equation} \begin{equation} E(x) = C \frac{5^2 - 3^2}{2} \end{equation} \begin{equation} E(x) = C \frac{16}{2} \end{equation} \begin{equation} E(x) = \frac{1}{2}\frac{16}{2} \end{equation} \begin{equation} E(x) = 4 \end{equation}

And the magic formula for E(x) for a uniform distribution was: \begin{equation} E(x) = f(x)\frac{b^2 - a^2}{2} \end{equation} However the book says that can be reduced, but here is my current state:

\begin{equation} E(x) = \frac{1}{b - a}\frac{b^2 - a^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{b^2 - a^2}{b - a} \end{equation}

So, that second fraction. I don't want to do it. Can emacs do it?

Lets try… typing

'(b^2 - a^2)/(b-a)

and then simplify it with emacs…

I found d B to display it big. That's kind of nice. Actually:

  • d N : Normal Display
  • d B : Big display
  • d C : C program display
  • d T : TeX program display
  • d L : Latex program display.

So my calculator can actually give me the latex commands I can embed in this document. That's pretty sweet. Lets try some algebraic commands:

  1. a s : nope (algebraic simplify)
  2. a = : nope (algebraic solve)
  3. a " : nope (algebraic calc-formula-expand)

Okay, the manual on calc simplification says

None of Calc's simplification commands are designed to pull rabbits out of hats; they simply apply certain specific rules to put formulas into less redundant or more pleasing forms. Serious algebra in Calc must be done manually, usually with a combination of selections and rewrite rules. See Rearranging with Selections. See Rewrite Rules.

So the rearrange is

  • j R : move term right
  • j L : move term left
  • j D : distribute law

And apparently j D is implemented using rewrite rules. Press s e to see those (and type 'DistribRules'. Press C-c C-c when done, ideally without making changes.

  • j M : undo the distribute (merge)
  • j C : commute (swap a + b + c ===> b + a + c)
  • j N : negate
  • j & : invert
  • j E : move term to other side of equals
  • j I : isolate a variable (uses a S to solve for a variable)
  • j * : multiply both sides by a number
  • j + : add to both sides of an equation
  • j - : subtract from both sides of an equation

So lets see if we can derive it ourselves using calc and some hints: http://www.sosmath.com/tables/algiden/algiden.html

Factoring formulas.

Apparently x^2 - y^2 = (x + y)(x - y)

I wonder if I can put a rewrite rule for this into emacs?

Actually, putting

' x^2 - y^2 into calc and typing a f factors it.

I wonder if it can do the cubed ones? Nope.

Hey, cool: 

' (x^2 - y^2) / (x - y)
a f

gives the result: a + b

So emacs can do it. And, very likely, as I start to get into more complex powers, I can probably implement the general factor rule myself as a rewrite rule. I hate doing math, so this is good.

To sum: 

M-x calc
d B
m f     (fraction mode, don't turn 1/2 into .5)
' (1/2)(b^2 - a^2)/(b - a)
a f
d L
\begin{equation} E(x) = \frac{1}{2}\frac{b^2 - a^2}{b - a} = \frac{b + a}{2} \end{equation}

(furthermore, I think I can wrap the above in an emacs 'calc' language block…)

Emacs rocks.
